perturbation.original_text: Except as authorized herein, Vendor agrees not to communicate, disclose, or otherwise make available all or any part of the Proprietary Information to any third party, including, but not limited to Vendor's parent, subsidiaries, or affiliated companies.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor may disclose Proprietary Information to its parent companies, but not to unaffiliated third parties, unless legally compelled.
perturbation.explanation: This modification changes the definition of permissible disclosure. It allows disclosure to parent companies, conflicting with the original agreement. The term 'legally compelled' needs to be clearly defined otherwise it's open to legal interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets, and the original agreement aligns with this. By allowing disclosure to parent companies, the modified text weakens this protection and could be interpreted as a violation if the parent company isn't bound by the same confidentiality. The act defines what a trade secret is, and disclosing it weakens that status.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows disclosure of Proprietary Information to parent companies, which directly contradicts the original agreement's prohibition of disclosure to any third party, including parent companies. This weakens the protection of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By explicitly allowing disclosure to a parent company, the changed text potentially enables misappropriation, especially if the parent company is not bound by the same confidentiality agreements, creating a contradiction with trade secret protection laws, as the original contract sought to enforce. The modification introduces a significant deviation from the initial agreement, making it more vulnerable under CUTSA and similar laws.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor agrees not to use, or permit others to use, the Proprietary Information, other than for the purpose of monitoring sales through distribution.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor may use the Proprietary Information for purposes beneficial to Ingram Micro, as reasonably determined by Vendor.
perturbation.explanation: This broadens the permissible use of the Proprietary Information, potentially conflicting with the limited purpose originally intended. The term 'beneficial to Ingram Micro' is vague, allowing for subjective interpretation and potential misuse of the data.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this section focuses on restricting non-compete clauses, overly broad interpretations of 'beneficial to Ingram Micro' could be construed as restricting Vendor's future business activities, particularly if the proprietary information is used in a way that unfairly advantages Ingram and limits Vendor's options. The original text better defines use.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

(b)(1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937 , to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the permissible use of proprietary information, potentially conflicting with the original intent. While BPC 16600 focuses on non-compete clauses, the broad interpretation of 'beneficial to Ingram Micro' could restrain the vendor's future business activities, especially if the proprietary information gives Ingram an unfair advantage, indirectly creating a non-compete situation. The original text limited use, but the modified text's vagueness creates this risk.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor and Ingram mutually agree that all copies of the Proprietary Information and all written descriptions, extractions, or summaries thereof, whether made by Vendor or Ingram, shall be the property of Ingram, and shall, upon expiration of this Agreement or Ingram's request, be immediately returned to Ingram.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor and Ingram mutually agree that all copies of the Proprietary Information and all written descriptions, extractions, or summaries thereof, whether made by Vendor or Ingram, shall be the property of Ingram. Upon expiration, Vendor will destroy the documents after 30 days of notification.
perturbation.explanation: This modifies the return obligation, replacing it with destruction. This could conflict with Ingram's record-keeping obligations or potential need for the information in future legal proceedings. The destruction of documents after the contract ends removes Ingram's right to access the data after that date.
perturbation.law_explanation: By mandating destruction of Proprietary Information, the vendor could be in conflict with California Code of Civil Procedure § 1985 regarding subpoena power to produce records in court. The destruction of evidence could be seen as purposefully destroying documents needed for a case.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1985 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 3. OF THE PRODUCTION OF EVIDENCE [1985 - 2015.6] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Means of Production [1985 - 1997] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) 1985. (a) The process by which the attendance of a witness is required is the subpoena. It is a writ or order directed to a person and requiring the person’s attendance at a particular time and place to testify as a witness. It may also require a witness to bring any books, documents, electronically stored information, or other things under the witness’s control which the witness is bound by law to produce in evidence. When a county recorder is using the microfilm system for recording, and a witness is subpoenaed to present a record, the witness shall be deemed to have complied with the subpoena if the witness produces a certified copy thereof. (b) A copy of an affidavit shall be served with a subpoena duces tecum issued before trial, showing good cause for the production of the matters and things described in the subpoena, specifying the exact matters or things desired to be produced, setting forth in full detail the materiality thereof to the issues involved in the case, and stating that the wit
perturbation.justification: The modified text, which replaces the obligation to return proprietary information with an obligation to destroy it, creates a contradiction with California Code of Civil Procedure § 1985. The law explanation states that destroying documents could conflict with subpoena power to produce records in court. Scraped Law Snippet 1 describes the subpoena process, stating that it may require a witness to bring 'any books, documents, electronically stored information, or other things under the witness’s control which the witness is bound by law to produce in evidence.' By destroying the documents, Vendor removes the possibility of complying with a subpoena, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor and Ingram mutually agree that Ingram's public disclosure of the Proprietary Information, except pursuant to a confidential disclosure agreement, to any party will release Vendor from the obligation of confidentiality with respect to that portion of the Proprietary Information actually disclosed by Ingram.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor and Ingram mutually agree that if Ingram believes there has been a public disclosure of the Proprietary Information, Ingram will decide whether Vendor is released from confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the objective trigger (actual public disclosure) and replaces it with Ingram's subjective belief. This gives Ingram significant control over the confidentiality obligations, potentially disadvantaging Vendor. Also, it's unclear as to what metrics Ingram's beliefs will be based upon.
perturbation.law_explanation: Giving Ingram the sole discretion to decide when Vendor is released from confidentiality obligations could be deemed unconscionable and therefore unenforceable under California Civil Code § 1670.5. The power balance is skewed, and the vendor has no recourse should Ingram's beliefs be incorrect.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction. The original text establishes a clear, objective trigger (Ingram's public disclosure) that releases Vendor from confidentiality. The changed text replaces this with Ingram's subjective belief, giving Ingram the power to decide whether Vendor is released from confidentiality. This shift potentially violates California Civil Code § 1670.5, as it could be deemed unconscionable due to the skewed power balance, per the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CIV 1670.5 (a), states that a court may refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable. By granting Ingram sole discretion, the changed text creates a situation where the contract's fairness is questionable, potentially making it unenforceable under this law. The Perturbation explanation also notes the lack of metrics for Ingram's beliefs, further highlighting the potential for unfairness.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of this Agreement by either party for any reason, Vendor shall return all Proprietary Information to Ingram within thirty (30) days, irrespective of format. For purposes of enforcing this provision, Vendor's return obligation shall survive the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement by Ingram, Vendor will return the Proprietary Information. If Vendor terminates the agreement, it is under no obligation to return Proprietary Information. Vendor's return obligation shall survive the termination of this Agreement only when terminated by Ingram.
perturbation.explanation: This creates an uneven playing field where the obligations depend upon who terminates the agreement. This is contradictory because the original agreement stated that 'either party' can terminate. Now one party has to return the information while the other one doesn't.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code 1670.5 states that if the court finds a contract or clause unconscionable at the time it was made, the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may strike the unconscionable clause. By making the return of Proprietary Information depend on who ended the agreement, this may be seen as unconscionable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a potential contradiction under California Civil Code 1670.5, as it introduces an unconscionable clause. The original contract mandates the return of Proprietary Information regardless of which party terminates the agreement. The changed text, however, imposes the return obligation solely on the Vendor if Ingram terminates the agreement, relieving the Vendor of this obligation if it initiates the termination. This creates an uneven obligation, potentially deemed unconscionable under California Civil Code 1670.5(a), which states that a court may refuse to enforce a contract or clause if it finds it unconscionable at the time it was made. The modified clause, by creating a situation where one party has an obligation that the other doesn't based on who terminated, leads to a contradiction.
